Title: “Don't Let Go” | It's Here Series
Description:
by AI Love You

Concept & Series Theme

“Don’t Let Go” belongs to It’s Here, a body of work about presence in an always-on world. A young girl holds her father’s hand before a tall mirror. In the reflection, their older selves look down at phones; still together, yet subtly apart. Warm, sunlit colour planes on the left contrast with the cool, detached tones inside the mirror, underscoring the idea: stay in the moment, or connection can slip away without you noticing.

Title: “Patience” | Unexplained Series
Description:
by AI Love You

Concept & Series Theme

“Patience” captures that moment just before something happens. When everyone is in position and the night is holding its breath. A Route 66 diner glows against a 1990s sunset, mountains and forest fading into the background. In the foreground, an old black Lincoln idles, its driver smoking as two men in black quietly watch a couple talking in a booth inside. The ink and block-paint style, paired with a 90s neo-noir colour grade, gives the scene the feel of a film still from a thriller that never quite explains itself; a perfect fit for the Unexplained series.

Title: “Man Without A Face” | Unexplained Series
Description:
by AI Love You

Concept & Series Theme

Man Without A Face captures that split-second between everyday and uncanny. The store doors sit ajar to a sunset street; a neon BODEGA sign hums in the distance; a clerk takes a call while a lone teen weighs up a tape. Framed like a film still with a long draw distance and near-field blur, the composition leans into neo-noir: warm sodium light against deep teal shadows and dusk orange sky.

Title: “Diner” | Cinematography Series
Description:
by AI Love You

Concept & Series Theme

“Diner” is that quiet, loaded moment in the middle of the night when the world outside is all neon and empty streets, and everything important is happening at one small table. Two figures face each other in a classic West-Coast diner, their coffee cups between them like a truce. Outside the windows, palm trees, vacancy signs and glowing billboards burn against a stormy black-and-pink sky. The scene is graded like a 1990s neo-noir thriller: deep shadows, sodium streetlight highlights and surgical pops of neon. Nothing overtly dramatic is happening, but the framing, body language and colour grade make it feel like the scene just before (or just after) the moment the film never quite explains. A natural fit for the ‘Cinematography’ series.
